q: Artificial satellites going around the earth at a constant height is given in the text as an example of which type of motion?
According to the textbook, which of the following is an explicit example of rotational motion?
a: Artificial satellites going around the earth at a constant height
The motion of the blades of a fan
A particle moving along a straight line path
An object moving in a circle at constant speed

q: How is a rigid body defined in Section 1.1 of the textbook?
a: A body that can change its shape depending on the external forces applied
A body that does not deform or change shape, where the distance between any two particles remains constant
A body whose particles all move in a straight line with constant linear velocity
A body that only undergoes uniform circular motion at all times

q: In rotational motion, all the particles constituting the rigid body undergo circular motion about what?
a: The outer edge of the body

q: Under what condition is a motion explicitly called "rotational motion under constant angular acceleration"?
a: When the angular velocity changes at a constant rate
When the linear velocity remains completely constant
When the angular velocity is equal to zero at all times
When the centripetal acceleration vector is zero

q: What symbol represents the initial angular speed (magnitude of angular velocity of the object at $t = 0$)?
Which formula represents the average angular speed $\bar{\omega}$ as shown in Equation (1.2)?
The kinematic equations for constant angular acceleration are analogous to those for linear motion, with the angular quantities replaced with what?
a: The radius of the circular path

q: In Table 1.1, what is the angular motion equation analogous to the linear motion equation $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2as$?
Based on Example 1.1, if a Ferris wheel starts from rest and reaches an angular velocity of $1.5\text{ rad s}^{-1}$ over a $10\text{ s}$ period, what is its constant angular acceleration?
According to Section 1.2, all particles of an object rotating around the axis of rotation move in what kind of path?
For a randomly shaped rigid body undergoing rotational motion, the particles move in circles lying on planes that have what relationship to the axis?
a: They are parallel to the axis
They are perpendicular to the axis
They are inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ to the axis
They do not intersect the axis

q: What is the relation between arc length $s$ and angular displacement $\theta$ as defined in the text?
Which of the following statements is true regarding the instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$ of a rotational object?
a: It is different for every particle comprising the object
It is the same for all the particles comprising the object
It increases with the radius of the particle's path
It decreases as the distance from the axis increases

q: Why is the linear velocity $v$ of a particle in a rotating object also called tangential velocity?
a: Because it points directly toward the center of rotation
Because the direction of linear velocity is tangent to the path
Because its magnitude is equal to the angular acceleration
Because it is perpendicular to the plane of rotation

q: What type of acceleration arises if the magnitude of the angular velocity of a rotating object changes?
What is the formula that relates tangential acceleration $a_T$ to angular acceleration \alpha?
The tangential acceleration of a rotating object is explicitly described as a measure of how fast what changes?
How is the direction of the angular velocity vector specified according to the textbook?
a: By the direction of tangential acceleration
By the direction of centripetal acceleration

q: When the angular velocity of a rotating object is decreasing, in what direction does the angular acceleration vector point?
a: In the same direction as the angular velocity
In the opposite direction to the angular velocity
Perpendicular to the angular velocity vector

q: An object that moves in a circle at a constant speed $v$ is said to experience what type of motion?
a: Rotational motion under constant linear acceleration

q: In uniform circular motion, what component of the velocity continuously changes to constitute an acceleration?
Where does the centripetal acceleration or radial acceleration vector $\vec{a}_C$ point?
a: Toward the centre of the circle
Away from the centre of the circle
Perpendicular to the axis of rotation along the z-axis

q: What geometric relationship exists between triangle CAB and the vector triangle of Figure 1.3(b) during the derivation of centripetal acceleration?
a: They are completely identical in size
They are right-angled triangles with the same hypotenuse
They are perpendicular to each other

q: What is the formula for centripetal acceleration $a_C$ in terms of linear speed $v$ and radius $r$?
What is the formula for centripetal acceleration $a_C$ when expressed in terms of angular velocity $\omega$ as shown in Equation (1.8)?
In uniform circular motion, what is the spatial relationship between the centripetal acceleration vector and the linear velocity vector at every point?
a: They are parallel to each other
They are perpendicular to each other
They are inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$

q: In nonuniform circular motion, what causes the object to experience a changing angular speed and angular acceleration?
a: Moving in a circular path with a varying speed
Moving in a straight line with constant velocity
The radius of the path continuously changing

What is the mathematical equation for the magnitude of the resultant acceleration $a$ in nonuniform circular motion?

The angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{a}_C$ is ____.
The direction of the angular velocity of a disc slowing down clockwise is ____.
In a ____ motion, the object just moves in a circle.
If the angular speed is decreasing, there is/are ____ acceleration(s).
